purpose: Provide an in-memory key-value store with optional TTL for application state, tests, sessions, and temporary computed results.

endpoints.description: Set a JSON value for a key with an optional TTL in seconds.
Retrieve a JSON value for a key when present and not expired.
Delete one key from the in-memory store.

sidecar_specification.purpose: Ephemeral JSON key-value storage with TTL support.

agent_usage.when_to_use: Use for temporary state, cache entries, fixtures, and workflow scratch data that can be lost on process restart.
agent_usage.when_not_to_use: Do not use when data must survive restarts, requires relational queries, or needs cross-process consistency.
agent_usage.example_workflow: 1) PUT /kv/{key} with value and optional ttl_seconds. 2) GET /kv/{key} during the same process lifetime. 3) DELETE when the value is no longer needed.

failure_modes.symptom: GET returns found false or 404 depending on route convention
failure_modes.recovery: Recompute or store the value again
Use a persistent storage module when durability is required

forbidden_behaviors.description: Must not write outside declared scope
Must not make external network calls
Must not persist values to disk

agent_notes.implementation_hint: Use a Map<string, { value: unknown; expiresAt: number | null }>. On get, delete expired entries before responding. Use setInterval with unref() to sweep expired entries every 60 seconds.
agent_notes.known_gotchas: TTL math should use Date.now() milliseconds and reject negative ttl_seconds. Stored objects should be cloned on set and get to avoid mutation through references.
agent_notes.preferred_approach: Create a MemoryKvStore class with set, get, delete, and sweepExpired methods and test it independently from HTTP routes.
agent_notes.avoid_approach: Do not use Redis, SQLite, local files, or process-global variables outside a single store instance.

test_cases.name: expired value is missing
test_cases.notes: Test should create an expired entry before reading.
